does: This paper introduces AdaptiveK, a sparse autoencoder variant that dynamically adjusts sparsity levels during inference based on input semantic complexity rather than using fixed sparsity constraints. The key innovation is leveraging linear probes to detect that context complexity is linearly encoded in LLM hidden states, then using this signal to guide how many features activate per input during SAE reconstruction. The authors validate their approach across ten language models, showing improvements over fixed-sparsity baselines across multiple metrics including reconstruction fidelity, explained variance, cosine similarity, and interpretability measures, while also reducing hyperparameter tuning requirements.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, extending recent efforts to decompose LLM activations into human-interpretable features. It responds to a known limitation of prior SAE methods that treat all inputs uniformly despite the variable difficulty of interpreting different semantic contexts. The paper connects to broader work on input-dependent allocation of model capacity and complexity measures in neural networks, as well as research showing that linguistic properties like syntax and semantics are linearly representable in transformer activations. The use of linear probes to extract complexity signals echoes prior interpretability work that discovers task-relevant information through simple linear functions.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward more efficient and targeted interpretation of LLMs by showing that adaptation mechanisms can improve representation quality. It suggests future work could apply similar complexity-driven adjustment to other interpretability methods beyond SAEs, or extend the approach to different model architectures and modalities. The finding that input complexity is linearly encoded could inspire broader investigations into how models allocate computational and representational resources dynamically. The method also enables more economical sparse autoencoders by reducing wasted capacity on simple inputs, potentially supporting real-time interpretability analysis. These insights may inform the design of intrinsically interpretable models and support development of more efficient adapter mechanisms in large models.
